coredata.dc:title: Properties and molecular structure of carbon quantum dots derived from empty fruit bunch biochar using a facile microwave-assisted method for the detection of Cu2+ ions
coredata.dc:creator: Kamarol Zaman, Alif Syafiq
Tan, Tong Ling
AP Chowmasundaram, Yamuna
Jamaludin, Norhanisah
Sadrolhosseini, Amir Reza
Rashid, Umer
Rashid, Suraya Abdul
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQD) have received considerable attention due to its advantageous characteristics of highly fluorescent nature and good biocompatibility. In this study, CQD were successfully prepared from empty fruit bunch (EFB) biochar, a renewable and sustainable resource using a facile microwave-assisted method in just minutes. The structures, morphology, and functional groups of CQD were analysed by HRTEM, FTIR and XPS analysis. CQD displayed bright blue emission upon exposure to UV irradiation. The maximum fluorescence emission of CQD was detected at 420 nm throughout the excitation wavelength 280 nm. To obtain optimum molecular structure of CQD, density functional theory (DFT) calculations were carried out using 6-31G(d) basis set which also includes a comparison of three exchange correlation functions such as B3LYP, CAM-B3LYP and wB97XD. These functionals were used to calculate the ground state molecular structure of CQD. The HOMO-LUMO gap obtained from 6 to 31G(d)/B3LYP was around 6.4 eV and is closer to the experimental band gap of 4.4 eV compared to CAM-B3LYP and wB97XD. The fluorescence quenching effect was observed when Cu2+ ions were added into CQD solution. The experiment results showed that the as-prepared CQD have a sensitive response to Cu2+ ions in the concentration range of 0–400 μM. This work not only provides insight into the properties and molecular structure of CQD obtained via a facile and scalable method, but also shows potential of a fluorescence sensor for metal ions detection.

head.ce:title: Properties and molecular structure of carbon quantum dots derived from empty fruit bunch biochar using a facile microwave-assisted method for the detection of Cu2+ ions

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQD) have received considerable attention due to its advantageous characteristics of highly fluorescent nature and good biocompatibility. In this study, CQD were successfully prepared from empty fruit bunch (EFB) biochar, a renewable and sustainable resource using a facile microwave-assisted method in just minutes. The structures, morphology, and functional groups of CQD were analysed by HRTEM, FTIR and XPS analysis. CQD displayed bright blue emission upon exposure to UV irradiation. The maximum fluorescence emission of CQD was detected at 420 nm throughout the excitation wavelength 280 nm. To obtain optimum molecular structure of CQD, density functional theory (DFT) calculations were carried out using 6-31G(d) basis set which also includes a comparison of three exchange correlation functions such as B3LYP, CAM-B3LYP and wB97XD. These functionals were used to calculate the ground state molecular structure of CQD. The HOMO-LUMO gap obtained from 6 to 31G(d)/B3LYP was around 6.4 eV and is closer to the experimental band gap of 4.4 eV compared to CAM-B3LYP and wB97XD. The fluorescence quenching effect was observed when Cu2+ ions were added into CQD solution. The experiment results showed that the as-prepared CQD have a sensitive response to Cu2+ ions in the concentration range of 0–400 μM. This work not only provides insight into the properties and molecular structure of CQD obtained via a facile and scalable method, but also shows potential of a fluorescence sensor for metal ions detection.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQD) are nanosized fragments of carbon nanomaterials with sizes typically less than 10 nm [1–4]. Being zero-dimensional nanostructures, they have unique electrical and optical properties as well as low-toxicity, good biocompatibility, and good water solubility [5–9]. These properties enable CQD to have versatile applications in optoelectronics, photocatalysis, bioimaging, biosensing, and heavy metal sensing [10–13]. Furthermore, to produce CQD, numerous methods have been reported which include two major categories, “bottom-up” and “top-down”. In general, the “bottom-up” pathway includes the synthesis of CQD from smaller precursors like carbohydrates, and citrates whereas in the “top-down” synthetic pathway, larger carbon compounds are used such as graphite sheets, and carbon nanotubes with possible modification during post-treatment steps [14].

CQD have been synthesized from various carbon precursors such as graphite or graphene oxide using modified Hummer's methods and hydrothermal routes [15,16]. Unfortunately, these methods of synthesising CQD uses chemicals that not only are hazardous but also toxic to the environment and requires long reaction times (3 days in the case of Hummer's method) to produce significant yields [17,18]. Although using acids can provide higher yields, it is still harmful to the environment and can be difficult to handle. Furthermore, hazardous risks of Hummer's method due to the addition of potassium permanganate and sulphuric acid which can cause explosions if not done correctly, are often underestimated [19].

It has also been reported that small molecules such as citric acid and glucose have been used to synthesize CQD which possess excellent optical properties and application [20,21]. However, these methods are still costly multi-step processes, harsh synthetic conditions and time consuming. Yongli Liu et al. have synthesized CQD from sodium citrate and polyacrylamide but the reaction time is 3 h and at 200 °C [22]. The process of producing CQD using a cheaper carbon precursor, lower reaction time and temperature is more desirable. For example, biochar which is a type of biomass obtained from pyrolysis of agrowaste, would be a better alternative and much more cost-effective carbon source and energy saving for producing CQD [23]. Currently, Malaysia produces a large amount of agrowaste biomass particularly oil palm empty fruit bunch (EFB) from the oil palm industry. Converting the biomass into useful product and further utilization will also support the global movement to develop sustainable technology and as one of many ways to reduce carbon footprint in order to lessen the impact of climate change on the planet [24,25]. Therefore, empty fruit bunch (EFB) biochar is chosen as the carbon precursor for this study.

Moreover, previous work have reported that at least 45 min at 250 °C is needed to produce CQD from EFB biochar using hydrothermal method [26]. For faster reaction time as compared to using a conventional oven for heat source, we have chosen microwave as the heating method due its uniform heating and steep temperature gradient. Zheng et al. have demonstrated that it is possible to prepare nitrogen functionalized CQD as low as 1 min with microwave [2]. This heating method can also be carried out with a larger volume. Microwave heating is also considered to be much more efficient compared to other methods due its heat transfer mechanism where it can interact directly with the sample matrix by ionic conductions and dipolar rotation whilst increasing the likelihood of a reaction to occur [27].

The “bottom up” formation of CQD has been proposed to be a series of processes consisting of cross-linking and chemical reaction of small molecules into bigger CQD fragments [28,29]. CQD formation from small molecules is generally easier to understand because one can control the properties of the CQD by controlling the parameters of the process and the type of precursors used. However, for “top down” approach which is “cutting” down of bulk material into smaller molecules poses a bigger challenge in understanding its formation. This would be particularly true for CQD prepared from biomass precursors as there are many oxygen moieties and surface states present that influence their optical properties.

Obtaining insights into the molecular structure of a CQD molecule is also important if the properties of CQD is to be understood. Density functional theory (DFT) is a very suitable method to predict or simulate some of the properties of CQD. The data obtained from DFT calculations can be compared with experimental data in order to validate whether both data are in agreement with each other. Appropriate choice of basis sets and exchange correlation functions can have a huge impact on the quality of the results [30,31]. Therefore, in this work, we report a greener alternative, cost-effective and less time-consuming process of producing CQD from EFB biochar using a facile microwave assisted process. The CQD was fully characterized and the molecular structure of the CQD was modelled using DFT. The performance of the CQD as fluorescence sensor with excellent biocompatibility and high sensitivity for Cu2+ ion was evaluated. The as prepared CQD showed a sensitive response to Cu2+ in the concentration range of 0–400 μM and the LOD were 0.42 μM, which presented potential applications in the water environment.

EFB biochar was provided by Pakar Go Green Sdn Bhd, Malaysia. Isopropanol (IPA) with 98% purity was purchased from Merck, Darmstadt, Germany. Copper II nitrate, trihydrate, Cu (NO3)2·3H2O (99%) was obtained from ChemAR. NaCI, KCI, MgCI2, CaCI2, Ba (NO3)2, Ni (NO3)2.6H2O, Zn (NO3)2.6H2O, FeSO4.7H2O were supplied from R& M chemicals. All chemicals were utilized as received without further purification. Deionized water was used throughout the experiment.

The natural carbon precursor used in this study was EFB biochar and the solvent used for the preparation was a mixture of IPA and distilled water; a mole fraction of 0.1 in 100 mL of total mixture was prepared. Next, 1 g of milled EFB biochar was added into the solution and stirred. Afterwards, the mixture was added into a Teflon vessel before being placed inside a microwave reactor. The temperature inside the microwave reactor was varied from 60 to 100 OC (maximum reactor temperature range) while the time was set at 5 min. No significant changes in the yield was obtained when the time was increased. A stirrer was also placed in the reactor and was set to 200 rpm. These process parameters were chosen to investigate the effect of temperature on the yield of CQD. After the heating process, the reactor was cooled down to room temperature. Consequently, the CQD/biochar mixture was filtered using filter paper and the supernatant liquid CQD was collected. CQD was further dialyzed using a dialysis tube (molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of 3.5 kDa) against deionized water for three days. The yield of CQD was calculated from the excess biochar subtracted from the initial weight of 1 g as shown in Equation 1.

A UV–visible absorption spectrum of the CQD in aqueous solution was recorded using UV spectrometer (UV–Vis, Lambda 35, PerkinElmer). The photoluminescent emission spectra was recorded using a photoluminescent setup (PL, LS 55, PerkinElmer) in aqueous solution. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the CQD were obtained by Shimadzu 6000 with Cu-Kα radiation at scanning rate of 1.2° min-1 from 0° to 80° to determine the phase purity. Fourier Transform Infrared (Thermo Nicolet 6700 FTIR) spectrum was also recorded to identify the functional groups or surface state present on the obtained CQD. Images of CQD were collected using High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope, HRTEM (FEI-Tecnai F20). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were carried out on a scanning X-ray microprobe PHI Quantera II (Ulvac-PHI, INC.) using a monochromatic Al-Kα (hv = 1486.6 eV) X-ray source. Note that these characterizations were carried out for CQD obtained from the highest yield. CQD in powder form was obtained by drying CQD solution in a sample vial. Raman spectroscopy measurement was carried out using Witec Alpha 300R with 532 nm laser. 5 drops of CQD solution was dried on a glass slide and was examined under the Raman microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Molecular modelling using density functional theory (DFT)
ce:section.ce:para: Geometry optimisation and frequency analysis for the proposed structure of CQD were executed using 6-31G basis set with Becke's three parameter with Lee–Yang–Parr non local electron correlation (B3LYP). Further optimisation using 6-31G(d) with B3LYP, Coulomb attenuating method B3LYP (CAM-B3LYP) and wB97XD exchange correlation together with integral equation formalism Polarizable Continuum Model (IEFPCM) for solvation effect in IPA [25]. Both CAM-B3LYP and wB97XD are range separated functionals with the aim to reduce errors when calculating long-range interactions and charge transfer [32,33]. The software used for this calculation were Gaussian09 and GaussView 5. The resultant theoretical IR spectrum generated from the software was compared with results from experimental FTIR to complement the chemical structure interpretation.

A standard stock solution of 0.01 M of Cu (II) was carried out by dissolving an appropriate amount of Cu (NO3)2 in 1 L of water and adjusting the volume to 5.0 mL in a volumetric flask. A series of concentration Cu2+ ion aqueous solution (2, 4, 40, 60, 80, 100, 200, 300 and 400 μM) was prepared in advance and added into aqueous solution containing the same concentration of CQD. For the sake of comparison, the volume of CQD solution was fixed to be 2 mL before the addition of Cu2+. All measurements were made at room temperature. The PL intensity was recorded from 300 to 500 nm by Microplate Reader (Infinite 200 PRO, Tecan) at the optimum excitation wavelength of 250 nm after a reaction had been carried out for 30 min. The fluorescent lifetime of the CQD was measured using a time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) spectrum (HORIBA Instruments Incorporated, Edison, NJ, USA). The fluorescence transients were fitted with triple-exponential function: τ = A1 exp (−t/τ1) + A2 exp (−t/τ2) + A3 exp (−t/τ3), where A1, A2, and A3 are amplitude constituents of the first, second and third decay exponents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of different metal ions on the fluorescence intensity of CQD
ce:section.ce:para: For the selectivity measurement of CQD towards Cu2+, NaCI, KCI, MgCI2, CaCI2, Ba(NO3)2, Ni(NO3)2.6H2O, Zn(NO3)2.6H2O, FeSO4.7H2O were used as metal ion salts. All metal ion salts were dissolved in deionized water with the appropriate amount and adjusted their concentrations to 80 μM. In a typical detection experiment, 2 mL of CQD solution was added to the solutions containing the calculated metal ions. The PL intensity was recorded from 300 to 500 nm by Microplate Reader (Infinite 200 PRO, Tecan) at the excitation wavelength of 250 nm after a reaction had been carried out for 30 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of temperature on CQD yield and optical properties
ce:section.ce:para: The process for obtaining CQD from EFB biochar in the microwave reactor was done by varying the heating temperature from 60°C to 100 °C while fixing the solvent concentration and reaction time at 0.1 mol fraction and 5 min, respectively. All the set temperatures were able to produce CQD but at different yields. The yield of CQD from the process is tabulated in Table 1 together with the observed pressures. Due to microwave uniform and fast heating, the process heats up to the targeted temperature in just under a minute.

The yield of CQD increased as the set temperature was increased up until 100 °C. This can be explained using vapour-liquid equilibrium (VLE) of IPA–water phase system. For temperatures between 60 and 80 °C, both IPA and water in the mixture are in liquid phase while at 90 °C and 100 °C, some of the liquid phase changes into vapour phase. With higher temperature, the yield of CQD improves due the reduction of the solvent surface tension between the solvent molecules [34–37]. This is due to the decrease of the cohesive force between the molecules of the solvents resulting in lower intermolecular interaction thus increasing CQD yield [35]. Therefore, it is believed that reducing surface tension of solvents will increase CQD yield. During the microwave heating, the water molecules as well as the IPA solvents will move through the solution based on the orientation of the electric field and thus, the rotation, friction, and collision of molecules will generate the heat. However, the energies of microwaves are not strong enough to cleave the chemical bonds, the microwave heating is only able to affect the molecular rotations. Therefore, microwave chemistry is not inducing chemical reactions by direct absorption of electromagnetic radiation but is based on the efficient heating of materials [36]. Note that microwave heating is rapid, and the system was observed to reach 100 °C in 2 min. Hence, it is assumed that CQD was obtained when IPA was still in the mixture (liquid form). Moreover, based on preliminary study, it was found that there was no formation of CQD (no fluorescence under UV-light irradiation) in the absence of IPA. From our previous reported study, isopropanol mainly acts as solvent to extract CQD from the bulk biochar and dispersed in the liquid medium during the hydrothermal treatment [38].

The absorption spectrum of CQD obtained from the process is represented in Fig. 1 a. The highest peak is at 206 nm which is contributed by the π-π* transition of the carbon core of CQD [39]. Another peak present in the spectra at 231 nm originated from the n-π* transition of the functional groups such as CO or COOH making up the surface states of CQD [40]. Both of these peaks are lower in terms of wavelength compared to the works of for example Pramanik et al. [41] and Zhengyu et al. [42] because of the different biomass precursors used and also the methods used for the synthesis. A previous bottom–up approach of producing CQD have shown that the π-π* transition at 208 nm which is close to ours and a slightly higher n-π* transition at 297 nm [43]. Furthermore, CQD size and edge states could also be responsible for these differences in the peak absorbance wavelength [44,45].

PL spectra at various excitation wavelengths, λex, were obtained from the CQD produced at 100 °C as shown in Fig. 1b. It is found that 280 nm λex produced the strongest emission wavelength, λem, with a peak at 420 nm, which gives out brilliant blue fluorescence when illuminated with a 365 nm UV lamp. This sample also appears slightly yellow in colour in normal daylight [40,46]. The PL spectrum exhibits an excitation-independent emission because the λem of different λex have relatively similar peak wavelengths and no significant peak shifts were observed as λex changes. This excitation-independent spectrum is most probably due to monodispersed CQD with a smaller size distributions [45]. The surface state also influences the optical properties of CQD whereby the CQD obtained from this process have oxygen moieties which is believed to have originated from the EFB biochar. Additionally, Ding et al. have shown that the emission or colour of CQD is governed by surface oxidation where the emission peaks redshift with increasing degree of surface oxidation [47]. Fig. 1c shows the PL spectra of IPA after microwave reactions, indicating no emission peak was found as the IPA mainly acts as solvent to extract CQD from the bulk biochar and dispersed in the liquid medium.

To investigate the chemical composition of the extracted CQD, FTIR and XPS analyses were performed on CQD prepared at 100 °C. Fig. a presents the XPS survey scan of CQD where C and O are the main elements that make up the entire structure of CQD. It is also evident that potassium (K), peaks are also detected in XPS wide scan spectrum labelled as K2s and K2p which is suspected to arise from raw EFB. The raw EFB usually consisted of high potassium content and the potassium are always present in the form of K+ in the biomass and show high affinity to water. Normally, the K+ ion could be removed using water washing where the K+ ion will likely react with OH− ion in the water. Thus, the presence of K+ ion in the CQD could be the remaining of potassium left in the EFB [48–51].

The high resolution C1s spectrum indicates that the CQD contain four chemical states at 284.6, 285.5, 286.5 and 288.1 eV as shown in Fig. 2 b [52–54]. The typical peaks are attributed to C–C/CC, C–O, CO and COOH bond respectively. It clearly indicates that CQDs were attached with hydroxyl, carboxyl, and carboxylic acid group, which are beneficial to the surface modification and shows good water solubility of CQD. The O1s, is shown in Fig. 2c, whereby the presence of oxygen species C–O (530.7 eV), CO (532.1 eV) and COOH (533.5 eV) is evident, where the XPS results reveal that the surface of the oxygen-containing functionalized CQD is possibly caused by the introduction of more oxygen-containing groups during the procedure of microwave reaction for the preparation of CQD [55].

Fig. 3 a shows the FTIR spectrum of CQD. The presence of CC stretching vibrational modes was confirmed by the slightly broad absorption band centred at 1640 cm−1 [41]. The existence of CC stretching mode confirms the presence of CQD. Furthermore, the IR absorption band in 1120–1310 cm−1 is the C–O stretching vibrational mode which further confirms the presence of carboxyl group. In addition, the band at 1380-1470 cm−1 and 2978 cm−1 is from the C–H (bending, wagging, scissoring) and stretching modes, respectively. Notably, the broad peak centred at 3270 cm−1 is the –OH stretching mode [56]. One of the reasons why –OH peak is broad, is due to the formation of hydrogen bond with the solvent.

The calculated FTIR spectrum from DFT calculations in Fig. 3b exhibits similar peaks and shape compared to experimental FTIR. The most obvious similarity is the combined CO and CC peak at 1640 cm−1 where DFT is able to predict the vibrational resolved spectrum very well and is in good comparison with experimental FTIR. However, DFT calculation with B3LYP functional have a poor prediction for the –OH vibration because B3LYP is not capable of calculating long-range interaction and will be further discussed in section 3.4 .

Raman spectrum analysis is very useful in characterising carbon nanomaterials as it is non-destructive and it is usually utilized when analysing graphene related materials [57]. Based on Fig. 3c, the disordered (D) band was observed at 1349 cm−1 with a full–width at half–maximum (FWHM) of about 90 cm−1, while the crystalline (G) band was observed at 1594 cm−1 with FWHM of about 50 cm−1. The quality of the as-prepared CQD is also confirmed by the ratio of the intensity of D band to G band or ID/IG with a value of 0.9137 indicating that the structure of CQD is fairly graphitic [58]. The surface of CQD contains oxygenic functional group as discussed above, which contributes to sp3 defects to the neighbouring sp2 of the carbon core of CQD. This could also explain the slightly broad and asymmetric D band [17,59]. Interestingly, the D band is lower than the G band which shows a degree of graphitization suspected to originate from the chemically stable molecular structure of EFB biochar itself. The typical X-ray diffraction (XRD) of CQD is shown in Fig. 3d. CQD show a single broad diffraction peaks centred around ~2θ~21.5°, which is attributed to the (002) lattice spacing of amorphous nature of carbon nanodots by the introduction of more oxygen-containing groups [60].

The surface morphology of CQD is further examined using HRTEM. Fig. 4 a reveals the almost spherical CQD which are uniformly dispersed with no apparent aggregation observed. The diameter of monodispersed CQD was calculated from micrographs of 100 dots taken randomly. The CQD size distribution obtained was in the range of 2.5–7.5 nm, with an average diameter of 4.5 nm, as shown in Fig. 4b. The lattice fringe was also identified as presented in Fig. 4c, with the d-spacing estimated to be 0.212 nm which corresponds to the {1 0 0} planes of graphitic carbon core [11,61–63]. Furthermore, to prove the CQD extracted from this process have a graphitic core, FFT image was generated (shown in the inset of Fig. 4c) which reveals the hexagonal structure of CQD [2,62]. Moreover, SAED pattern as exhibited in Fig. 4d shows diffuse rings indicating the polycrystalline nature of the CQD film [64,65].

Molecular modelling based on DFT was set up and performed according to the characterisation results and was used to find the optimum molecular structure of CQD. The proposed structure for CQD is a single-layer graphene as the carbon core, with the edges terminated with oxygen species as the functional groups such as –OH, CO and COOH and also saturated with hydrogen atoms [66]. This consideration is taken based on the FTIR characterisation results as discussed in section 3.2 . The proposed molecular structure of CQD was optimized in IPA, using 6-31G(d) basis sets with B3LYP, CAM-B3LYP and wB97XD exchange correlation functionals to compare the solvation effect of IPA using IEFPCM. Fig. 5 a presents the optimized structure of CQD at 6-31G(d)/B3LYP level of theory together with the dipole moment vector in Fig. 5b.

A comparison of a few selected parameters before and after geometry optimisation is tabulated in Table 2 . The total energy of −1832.05 Hartree (−49852.62 eV) was found to be the lowest when B3LYP functional is used implying that the most stable structure was achieved. Frequency analyses were also done at the same level of theory, together with CAM-B3LYP and wB97XD. No imaginary frequencies were found indicating the structure is at its minimum energy and the most stable. The values for bond length and bond angle were taken from the innermost carbon ring to elucidate that the proposed CQD structure has been minimized to its ground state form. The values obtained for bond lengths are in agreement with the works of Wang et al. [67].

From the DFT findings, all three functionals are suitable for finding electronic energy of the CQD structure at the ground state geometry. Furthermore, the dipole moments calculated with wB97XD have better accuracy than CAM-B3LYP even though both are range-separated functionals. B3LYP is still a reliable functional in predicting the dipole moments and turns out to be higher in value compared to CAM-B3LYP as shown in Table 2. It has also been demonstrated by Zara et al. that wB97XD does have slightly better accuracy compared to CAM-B3LYP [68]. However, the dipole moments calculation can change with increasing solvent polarity and this study was only carried out in IPA [69].

The highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy difference between different functionals is tabulated in Table 3 . The HOMO is apparently located on the carbonyl group and LUMO is at the carboxyl group at atom O2 and O36 respectively. This indicates that the oxygen species is the main contributor to the molecular orbitals and belongs to the electronic transition of n–π*.

The smallest HOMO-LUMO gap of 6.38 eV was achieved using B3LYP. The gap seems to be higher or overestimated with CAM-B3LYP and wB97XD functionals compared to B3LYP. In addition, the experimental band gap was also calculated to be 4.4 eV as shown in Fig. 6 . The energy band gap (Eg) value was calculated by using Tauc's relation, αhν = A(hν – Eg)n, where a is the absorption coefficient, hν is the photon energy, Eg is the optical band gap, and n equals 1/2 for direct allowed transition [70]. The HOMO-LUMO gap is often considered to be the band gap and the prediction from B3LYP is not too far from the experimental data as compared to CAM-B3LYP and wB97XD [25]. Due to the presence of π-conjugation in CQD molecules, the band gap is typically considerably smaller in energy than molecular fundamental gap (HOMO-LUMO) [71]. Overall, the B3LYP exchange correlation function is the most suitable method to study CQD in this work.

Furthermore, the HOMO and LUMO of CQD structure obtained at 6-31G(d)/B3LYP level of theory are visualized in Fig. 7 a–b. To visualize the reactive sites of CQD structure, molecular electrostatic surface potential map (MESP) is plotted on the total electron density as shown in Fig. 7c. MESP is also useful for predicting which site of the molecule is prone to nucleophilic or electrophilic attack [24,62]. The MESP is colour coded from red to blue which corresponds to negative and positive sites respectively. The map reveals that the most possible sites for nucleophilic attack are H41, H55 and H56 which all belongs to hydroxyl group while the sites susceptible for electrophilic attack are O2, O35 and O39 which belongs to carbonyl and carboxyl group. The red region is rich in electron and electron transfer mechanism will more likely to occur on these sites. This MESP from DFT results could possibly explain the electron transfer mechanism in photocatalysis processes.

In a recent work where CQD aided a photocatalysis process, the CQD acts as electron acceptor to trap the electrons and hinder electron-hole recombination leaving more charge carriers to form reactive species such as OH• and O2 •− which can reduce pollution. The presence of unpaired electron on the oxygen atom makes OH• a very reactive species with a mean lifetime estimated as only a few nanoseconds in water. It destroys most of organic and organometallic pollutants until total mineralization and therefore, OH• usually reduces greenhouse gases and pollutants [72].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensitivity measurement of CQD towards Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: In the present work, the potential of using CQD as fluorescence probes for copper metal ion was explored in detail by measuring the fluorescence changes of the developed CQD. The fluorescence response of CQD was studied towards Cu2+ in a concentration range of 0–400 μM to investigate the fluorescence quenching mechanism of CQD. As shown in Fig. 8 a, a gradual decrease in CQD emission intensity without any change in the peak position was observed on addition of increasing concentration of Cu2+. When the concentration of Cu2+ ions reached 400 μM, the fluorescence intensity of CQD solution became very weak and its value reached to a fixed level. The percentage quenching can be expressed as Q % = [(I0–I)/I0] x 100 where I and I0 are the fluorescence intensities of the CQD in the presence and absence of the Cu2+, respectively. The quenching percentage of ~89% is observed by 400 μM Cu2+ while ~9% at very low Cu2+ concentration of 2 μM. Quenching percentage of Cu2+ on the CQD fluorescence emission is found to be proportional to the concentration of Cu2+.The fluorescence emission of CQD could be quenched by Cu2+ ion due to the special coordination interaction between Cu2+ ion and the carboxyl and hydroxy groups on the surface of CQD. This may contribute to intramolecular nonradiative recombination of charge carriers that involves partial transfer of an electron in the excited state to the d orbital of Cu2+ [73].

Fig. 8b shows a linear relationship (R2 = 0.9927) in the range of Cu2+ concentration from 0 to 400 μM. The fluorescence quenching caused by Cu2+ can be well described by the Stern-Volmer equation:
(1)
F 0
/F =
K sv
( Q )
+ C

Where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity in the absence and presence of quencher Cu2+, respectively, Ksv is the Stern-Volmer constant representing the affinity between CQD and analyte Cu2+, Q is the analyte concentration, and C is a constant (~1). The Stern-Volmer constant (Ksv), was calculated in the range for a linear regression equation plotted against Cu2+ concentration in μM (R2 = 0.9927). Under the current experimental conditions, the theoretical limit of detection (LOD) of Cu2+ was estimated to be 0.42 μM based on the plot change in fluorescence intensities against Cu2+ concentration for linear equation. This experimental data certainly demonstrate that the effective energy transfer happens between CQD and Cu2+ in the solution leading to increased fluorescence quenching. The limit of detection is calculated using:
(2)
LOD =
3.3 S
b
/k
where Sb is the standard deviation of the corrected blank signals of the CQD and k is the slope of the calibration curve. The CQD-Cu prepared in the work showed a relatively lower limit of detection when compared with other reported literatures which detected Cu2+, which validate the efficacy of this study (Table 4 ). Additionally, in most previous studies, CQD were synthesized using harsh strong chemical with longer production time. However, in this work, CQD were produced via a facile and cost-effective approach using natural resources (EFB) which turned out to have a remarkable sensing potential for Cu2+ in water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence selectivity and quenching mechanism of CQD towards Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: In the sensing system, selectivity was carried out to evaluate the applicability of CQD for Cu2+ determination. The impact of different metal ions including Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Ni2+, Zn2+ and Fe2+ on the PL intensity of CQD was studied. As shown in Fig. 9 a, the experimental results indicated that the CQD exhibited the highest selectivity for Cu2+ determination where the Cu2+ could effectively quench the fluorescence of CQD; while the other metal ions displayed a lower or negligible effect on their fluorescence intensities. The high fluorescence quenching effect of Cu2+ could originate from the fast electron transfer between Cu2+ and oxygen-rich groups on the surface of CQD. Basically, the oxygen functional groups on the surfaces of CQD provide not only the water solubility property, but also to their strong interaction with metal ions. The high selectivity of CQD upon Cu2+ could be attributed to the strong coordination between Cu2+ and the hydroxyl functional groups on the surface of CQD. For the coordination interaction, the electrons in the excited state of CQD could be transferred to the unfilled 3d orbitals of Cu2+, facilitating the nonradiative electron/hole recombination, which results in significant fluorescence quenching [7]. Besides, it was observed that the addition of Ba2+, Zn2+, Fe2+ and Cu2+ reduces the intensity of CQD whereby the effect of other cations is negligible. With the same type of CQD, different ions can be detected as reported in Refs. [80–82]. To further investigate the interference of other metal ions with Cu2+, a constant concentration of each metal ions was added into Cu2+ ions containing CQD and the PL spectrum was measured. Fig. 9 b shows that the coexistence of other metal ion with Cu2+ has no significance effect on the fluorescence sensing of Cu2+ as the fluorescence intensity is similar as for the solution containing Cu2+ only. The quenching effect of Fe2+ on Cu-CQD shows a minimal diminish but it is insignificant. Therefore, CQD can be used for selective fluorescence of Cu2+ ions in water sample.

To prove the fluorescence quenching mechanism, time-correlated single photon counting fluorescence decay times was used to investigate the charge transfer in the exciton recombination process of CQD in the presence and absence of Cu2+. The fluorescence lifetime curve of CQD and CQD-Cu are shown in Fig. 9 c. The lifetime data of CQD were fitted with a triple-exponential function as follows:
τ =
A 1
exp
( - t/
τ 1
)
+
A 2
exp
( - t/
τ 2
)
+
A 3
exp
( - t/
τ 3
)
where A1, A2, and A3 are amplitude constituent of the first, second and third decay exponents [38]. The decay curve consisted of a rapid constituent (τ1) and two slow constituents (τ2 and τ3). The average lifetime of CQD was decreased from 5.23 ns (τ1 = 0.0409 ns, τ2 = 2.1023 ns, τ3 = 10.6167 ns) to 0.74 ns (τ1 = 0.0131 ns, τ2 = 2.3855 ns, τ3 = 7.4813 ns) before and after adding Cu2+ to the CQD solution. The significantly reduced lifetime indicates the occurrence of dynamic quenching and confirms the electron transfer between Cu2+ and CQD contributes to the fluorescence quenching [5]. Fluorescence of CQD show selective response to Cu2+, implying the potential of CQD as a fluorescence probe for the detection of Cu2+. For further investigate the coordination interactions between CQD with the Cu2+, the FTIR peaks of CQD and CQD-Cu were determined to detect the changes of the stretching vibrations of the functional groups as shown in Fig. 9 d. The functional groups have a significant effect on the fluorescent behavior of CQD. From the FTIR spectra shown, the stretching vibrations of C–O–C at 1240 cm−1 in the CQD-Cu disappeared, suggesting the binding between the CQD and Cu2+ ions.

In summary, CQD were successfully obtained through a simple and green microwave-assisted synthesis method by using EFB as the carbon source and IPA as the solvent. The reaction temperatures varied from 60 to 100 °C was studied to reveal their effects on the yield of the CQD. The highest yield for this process of around 15% was obtained at 100 °C. HRTEM indicates all the as-prepared CQD are monodispersed with an average particle diameter of 4.5 nm. The prepared CQD showed good fluorescence properties and water solubility due to its functionality. The highest intensity PL spectrum was found at 280 nm excitation wavelength and the peak wavelengths are excitation energy independent. Theoretical FTIR and HOMO-LUMO gap values were found to be in reasonable agreement with experimental results. The B3LYP exchange correlation function was found to be the most suitable in this study to find the ground state structure of CQD. The fluorescent CQD presented outstanding sensitivity and were successfully applied for quantitative detection of Cu2+ in aqueous solutions with a linear detection range of 0–400 μM and a detection limit of 0.42 μM. As a result, the microwave heating technology has allowed the rapid production of CQD in minutes instead of hours with the potential to be scaled up for the production of CQD in the future.
